title: Standing waves on a string: why only the harmonics fit
scenario: A string of length $L = 1$ m, fixed at both ends, carries transverse waves at speed $v = 120$ m/s. It cannot vibrate at just any frequency — only at a discrete ladder of **standing waves**, the harmonics $f_n = nv/2L$@{wave-standing-frequency}. The $n$-th harmonic has $n$ half-wavelengths between the walls and $n-1$ interior nodes that never move. Drag the mode number: the shape changes, the nodes stay pinned, and the frequency climbs in exact integer steps. *Why* only integers? Because the ends are pinned — and that boundary condition is what quantizes the modes.

assumptions.claim: An ideal string fixed rigidly at both ends, so there is a displacement node at each end at all times.
The wave speed $v$ is constant (a uniform string under constant tension); damping is neglected, so the modes are sharp.

steps.label: What the algebra-based course hands you (a harmonic series)
steps.prose: A string fixed at both ends can only vibrate at a discrete set of frequencies — the fundamental $f_1 = v/2L$ and its integer multiples (the harmonics). You are handed the series as a rule, with no account of *why* only whole-number multiples appear and not, say, $1.5\,f_1$.

steps.label: The string obeys the wave equation; its standing solutions separate
steps.latex: \partial_{tt} y = v^2\,\partial_{xx} y \ \Longrightarrow\ y(x,t) = A\sin(kx)\cos(\omega t),\quad \omega = vk
steps.prose: Every small piece of the string obeys the wave equation. Looking for solutions that oscillate in place — a fixed shape times a time wobble — gives $y = A\sin(kx)\cos(\omega t)$, which solves it whenever $\omega = vk$. So far $k$ could be anything.

steps.label: The fixed ends quantize k — only integer modes fit
steps.prose: Now impose the physics: the string is pinned at both ends, so $y=0$ there at all times. $y(0)=0$ is automatic; $y(L)=0$ demands $\sin(kL)=0$, which is only true when $kL$ is a whole multiple of $\pi$. *That* is why $n$ must be an integer — the boundary conditions select a discrete ladder of modes. An integer number of half-wavelengths fits the length.

steps.label: The harmonic series falls out
steps.prose: Each allowed $k_n$ gives a frequency $f_n = nv/2L$ and wavelength $\lambda_n = 2L/n$ — the memorized series, now derived. Drag the mode slider: each harmonic adds one more antinode, the nodes (always-still points) stay pinned, and the frequency climbs in exact integer steps.

proof.heading: The harmonics come from the wave equation plus the fixed ends — the boundary conditions quantize the modes, and $f_n = nv/2L$ falls out.
proof.detail: check $\partial_{tt}y = v^2\partial_{xx}y$; fixed ends $\sin(kL)=0$; standing = two travelling waves; $f_n = nv/2L$; $\lambda_n = 2L/n$

checks.claim: The mode solves the wave equation $\partial_{tt} y = v^2\,\partial_{xx} y$ — back-substituting $y = A\sin(kx)\cos(\omega t)$ leaves zero, with $\omega = vk$.
The string is pinned at both ends: $y(0)=0$ automatically, and $y(L)=0$ forces $\sin(kL)=\sin(n\pi)=0$ — the boundary condition that quantizes the modes to integer $n$.
A standing wave is two counter-propagating travelling waves superposed: $\tfrac12 A[\sin(kx-\omega t)+\sin(kx+\omega t)] = A\sin(kx)\cos(\omega t)$.
The harmonic frequencies fall out: $f_n = \omega_n/2\pi = nv/(2L)$.
The wavelengths are $\lambda_n = 2\pi/k_n = 2L/n$ — an integer number of half-wavelengths fits the length.

misconception.claim: A string can be made to vibrate as a standing wave at any frequency you choose.
misconception.refuted_by: It cannot. The fixed ends force a node at each end, so only shapes with a whole number of half-wavelengths between them survive — the harmonics $f_n = nv/2L$. Drive the string at some other frequency and the reflections off the ends no longer line up: the wave interferes with itself and dies out instead of building a steady standing pattern. This is the same quantization that, one domain over, restricts an electron in a box to discrete energies — boundary conditions select a discrete ladder.

practice.prompt: A string of length $L = 1$ m, fixed at both ends, has wave speed $v = 120$ m/s. What is its fundamental frequency $f_1$?

choices.misconception: Used $v/L$ — forgot the factor of $2$. The fundamental fits only *half* a wavelength between the walls, so $\lambda_1 = 2L$ and $f_1 = v/2L$.
That is $v/4L$, the fundamental of a pipe closed at *one* end (where $\lambda_1 = 4L$). A string fixed at *both* ends has $\lambda_1 = 2L$, so $f_1 = v/2L$.
